data.title: Worried About Tariff Passthrough Onto Prices? So Are Business Execs
data.description:
By Kevin Foster, Aaron Jalca and Brent Meyer

Over the past couple of months, newswires have focused on the potential for elevated tariff rates to feed through into higher inflation and potentially affect output growth as well. Indeed, Chair Powell, in his last post-FOMC meeting press conference said, "What looks likely, given the scope and scale of the tariffs, is that… the risks to higher inflation, higher unemployment have increased."

Recent research from economists at the Atlanta Fed suggests that if firms are able to pass through all the costs of tariffs, retail prices would increase significantly - as much as 1.6 percent (depending on how effective tariff rates evolve from here).

And even at a 50 percent passthrough rate, the impact on prices would be large enough to be felt in the aggregate (0.8 percent increase in retail prices). How plausible is full passthrough? Going back to the last episode with rising tariffs in 2018, research showed that the cost of the tariffs was almost entirely passed through onto domestic prices.

Rising tariff rates pose threats to the real side of the economy as well. Another highly cited academic paper on tariffs points out that in a simple supply and demand model, "higher prices also reduce demand by domestic consumers."

In this environment, where policy changes lead to sharp increases in costs for many firms, we were curious about how firms would respond, especially in light of a potential reduction in demand that typically accompanies a price hike.

So, we turned to the Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey (BIE), a monthly survey of Sixth District firms that is well positioned to ask timely questions on economic conditions facing firms. In gathering information for the April 2025 BIE survey, we asked firms about their ability to pass through increased costs caused by a new economic policy without a resulting reduction in demand.

To that end, we posed two questions to firms about their ability to pass through a hypothetical cost increase to consumers. One of the questions was, "Suppose a new economic policy causes unit costs for your main good or service to increase by [10 or 25] percent, effective immediately. Based on current levels of demand for your main good or service, how much of that cost increase would your firm be able to pass through to customers?"

The survey was in fielded from April 7 through April 18, at a time when 10 percent was the baseline tariff for almost all countries, and 25 percent was the tariff on all foreign-made autos and auto parts. Some countries or regions, including China and the European Union, had much higher tariffs at the time.

The interesting twist in this line of questioning is the inclusion of the phrase "Based on current levels of demand." The interpretation here is that firms are telling us how much of the cost increase they would be able to pass through to customers before it had a negative impact on demand for that good or service.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey • Download data

Although a diversity of views is apparent, on average firms tell us they expect to be able to pass through 51.1 percent of a 10 percent cost increase, and 47.3 percent of a 25 percent cost increase, without reducing current levels of demand. A closer look at the responses reveals a wide range of expected passthrough rates.

Compared to the 2018 episode, where research suggests nearly full passthrough of costs into prices, our results suggest many firms believe their customers are price-sensitive enough this time around (perhaps owing to the recent inflationary surge that isn't too far in the rearview mirror) that they cannot pass through the entire cost increase without reducing demand. A natural next step, then, is to evaluate these responses in the context of firms' perceptions of their current demand.

Figure 2 plots the distribution of firms' passthrough percentages by their strength of sales revenue growth relative to "normal." Viewing this distribution reveals that firms with about normal or above-normal sales levels expect to pass through a much higher percentage of the cost increase compared to those firms with lower-than-normal sales levels.

For context, half of firms reported having below-normal sales levels, 38 percent reported about-normal sales levels, and 12 percent reported greater-than-normal sales levels.

On average, firms that reported "much less than normal" or "somewhat less than normal" sales levels expect to pass through 45.6 percent of the 10 percent cost increase, firms that report "about normal" sales levels expect to pass through a little more than half of the cost increase, and firms reporting "somewhat" or "much" greater than normal sales expect to pass through nearly two-thirds of a 10 percent cost increase. Results were similar for the 25 percent policy-related cost increase question.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey

To add further context to these results, we can look at the time series behavior of firms' quantitative sales gaps. Figure 3 shows that firms, on average, see sales levels flagging relative to normal, on the order of roughly 8 percentage points in the red.

Importantly, at the outset of the trade tensions and tariffs episode of 2018–19, firms perceived their sales levels to be about "normal" (in other words, their quantitative average sales gap was near 0 percent).

Given the relationship firms' passthrough rates have to their current levels of demand, we can infer that because perceived demand is weaker now than in the prior period of trade and tariff tensions, firms will be more hesitant to fully pass through tariff-related cost increases.

Source: Atlanta Fed's Business Inflation Expectations survey • Download data

In sum, firms with about normal or greater-than-normal sales expect to be able to pass through more of the cost increases while maintaining the same levels of demand for their goods or services, as figure 2 shows. And figure 3 shows us that those firms are more likely to be larger firms, due to their smaller sales gap compared to "normal."

In the aggregate, business executives see their current sales levels as about 8 percentage points below "normal," which is much weaker than firms' relative position entering 2018. In this environment, firms on average anticipate passing through a little more than half of a 10 percent cost increase without damaging demand.

It's not yet clear where the average tariff rate will ultimately settle, or how firms' passthrough rates will evolve from here. However, it does appear that most firms anticipate sacrificing demand should they choose to fully pass a tariff-related cost increase on to customers.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rates Spark: Global Markets Brace For U.S. Payrolls
data.description:
By Michiel Tukker

Markets are bracing for a weak US payrolls number

Friday is all about the US payroll numbers, which consensus expects to fall from 177k to 125k, thereby already positioning for a cooling jobs market. The whisper number on Bloomberg turned more pessimistic after this week’s data, with only 111k expected. US rate dynamics are more complex than in the eurozone, with different forces acting in opposing directions. Apart from growth worries, there are also inflation and fiscal pressures to consider.

A weak US payroll number should place more emphasis on the recession risk narrative and push yields down across the curve. But both the front end of the curve and the back end can only go so far. Inflation risks limit the Fed's ability to ease in the near term, and the ongoing discussions on Trump’s tax bill keep the back end higher. Whilst the spillovers to euro rates from the US seemed limited this week, we do think a bad US payroll number can hit global market sentiment and weigh down the back end of the euro curve.

The belly of the curve can outperform amid uncertainty

The European Central Bank cut by 25bp on Thursday, as widely expected, but left markets guessing about the timing of the next move. The press conference had a more hawkish tone than markets were positioned for, and now the next cut is only fully priced in by the fourth quarter. The bigger picture hasn't changed, however, and in our base case, we expect more range-bound trading centred around a landing zone of 1.75% for the ECB.

The belly of the curve could play an important role ahead, as also signified by the 5Y point underperforming other tenors yesterday. The fact is that inflation still remains above target, which means the ECB’s hands are not entirely free to cut rates if things turn for the worse. Meanwhile, the price impact due to Trump’s trade policies is hard to predict. As such, we could see the belly of the curve outperform if eurozone growth concerns flare up again. The long end of the curve is more anchored due to German spending expectations and has less room to come down.

Friday's events and market views

US payroll numbers later in the day will be the highlight, whereby the unemployment rate is expected to remain stable at 4.2%. From the eurozone, we first get retail sales, but given the numbers are from April, we don’t expect these to be a market mover.

More interesting may be the various ECB speakers slated. Holzmann from Austria will undoubtedly voice more hawkish comments, whilst Portugal's Centeno may voice more dovish sounds. Going forward, we could see more disagreement within the Governing Council about the ECB’s next steps.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Vertical SaaS At Scale: Intapp's Next Chapter
data.description:
Intapp (NASDAQ:INTA) operates in the vertical SaaS space, which naturally comes with a more limited total addressable market compared to broader horizontal SaaS platforms. Its customer base is niche but may be more sensitive to macroeconomic slowdowns, especially in discretionary tech spending. Additionally, as the company matures, its growth rates have begun to normalize, raising questions about how much further expansion is possible without significant market share gains or product innovation. Let us dive in to see where Intapp is headed.

Continued Topline Growth in SaaS and Cloud

Intapp provides SaaS solutions to workflows in legal and financial businesses with higher security requirements. There are several module products across business development, compliance and internal operations offered through its OnePlace platform. DealCloud, one of its flagship modules, is a combination of CRM and deal management solutions, tailored for specific needs typical in financial firms. The solutions are very focused and niche for capital markets, but also applicable for legal, accounting and consulting domains.

Intapp continues its transition into a maturing vertical SaaS business, with Q3 FY2025 results highlighting continued momentum in its cloud offerings despite a deceleration in overall revenue growth. Total operating revenue grew 16.6% YoY, with roughly 65% coming from SaaS operations. SaaS revenue itself grew 28% YoY. The remaining revenue comes from maintenance and support, on-premise solutions and services (the part that is less cloud delivered as well and expected to be lower margin than SaaS).

Cloud Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR) was also up 28% YoY. The cloud delivery encompasses most of the SaaS offerings along with AI enhanced tools (like Intapp Assist) or data services (fair amount of overlap with the SaaS offerings). The continued topline growth in SaaS and cloud segments reflects a move away from license and professional services revenue towards more predictable and higher margin recurring revenue. The trailing twelve-month cloud Net Revenue Retention (NRR) was also reported as a healthy 119%.

Overall, while the deceleration from ~30% growth in 2023 to ~20% in 2024 and 16.6% now indicates plateauing revenues as Intapp matures, the earnings quality is improving with encouraging topline growth in SaaS and cloud as seen earlier. There are quite a few positives in the retention metrics and growth quality to support a buy thesis, despite the plateauing revenue.

FY2025 guidance projects $331.3 million in SaaS revenue at the mid-point, showing ongoing strength and continued momentum in its cloud transition. The company also expects a non-GAAP EPS of $0.89 (midpoint) for the year. Full-year guidance for FY2026 will be interested in the face of discretionary IT spending cuts.

Free Cash Flow Positive Even at Breakeven

Intapp’s growing gross margins approaching 75% show the scalability typical of good SaaS businesses, especially as the revenue mix shifts toward higher margin cloud subscriptions. However, the fact that EBITDA remains around breakeven suggests that the company is still reinvesting heavily in sales, R&D and customer acquisition to drive growth, rather than prioritizing short-term profitability. This is acceptable for a vertical SaaS company during scale up phases, but I am a bit worried about plateauing revenue showing some signals of potential saturation (although with earning quality improvements due to cloud earnings growth). If the gross margins and operational efficiencies don't improve further, EBITDA improvements will be pressurized. I will also keep topline growth as a factor to watch.

Intapp reported a free cash flow of ~$33m in Q3 FY2025 (FCF to revenue ratio of ~25%). This despite EBITDA being just marginally around breakeven. This could be attributed to cost and CapEx discipline, which is both good and bad news. The good being obvious discipline and the bad is that despite the discipline, EBITDA is not materially positive, raising concerns around future profitability.

Reasonable Valuation for Intapp's Positioning

For valuation analysis, I chose Veeva Systems and nCino as benchmarks for Intapp. While being in the vertical SaaS space, they are in different stages of growth and profitability. Veeva, trading at a premium forward multiple of 12.7x EV/Revenue, is a mature and highly profitable player in life sciences vertical SaaS. On the other hand, nCino (focused on financial SaaS), with a lower forward multiple of 5.3x EV/Revenue, is in its early stages of growth and profitability. Intapp, trading at a forward ratio of 8.3x EV/Revenue, is between Veeva and nCino in terms of profitability and size. The stage versus valuation numbers mostly look in line considering the three peers' range.

Despite recent valuation contractions, the multiple for Intapp is still around the higher range of its history. However, that is not concerning as gross margins are improving, and the bottom line is showing signs of breaking even with good quality earnings improvement. In fact, the comparison with Veeva shows that if Intapp can scale and grow further, there is valuation multiple expansion potential still left. Despite limitations of the total addressable market for vertical SaaS versus horizontal SaaS.

Next Steps for Intapp

The thesis so far points to the margins, top line and relative valuations reflecting fairly on share prices as of today, with its cautions and positives. Where Intapp goes from here is therefore dependent on the fundamental strength of its offerings and immediate macro factors.

Workflows in legal and finance use cases are particularly sensitive to compliance requirements and have higher security needs. It is a niche requirement and Intapp's single focus helps cater to solutions demanded by the specific segment. Generic workflow tools will need more customization to get into the space. Intapp's specialization protects some sort of moat for its offerings. However, it is not a wide moat - there is competition from several disruptive smaller start-ups. Even bigger players have the capability and resources to get in over time. Existing competition addresses parts of the whole solution provided by Intapp, in a fragmented and generic way. Salesforce for CRM, Thomson Reuter's Elite legal back office suite and iManage's document and risk management solutions are examples of generic part solutions to the overall problem.

The SaaS and cloud emphasis is also supportive for long-term growth at healthy margins. Embedding AI into workflows can drive deeper customer engagement and enhance pricing power. Intapp identifies a Serviceable Addressable Market (SAM) of $15b today (and growing, from $9.6b in 2021) in its investor presentation, May 2025. Although I have highlighted the limited target market for vertical SaaS, today Intapp has penetrated only ~3% of the SAM. That shows lots of potential to grow is execution backs the promise.

Overall, Intapp has no clear long-term moat, but its vertical specialization and switching cost barriers mean better customer retention, if not acquisition. The recent emphasis on tighter regulatory scrutiny and compliance demands for enterprises (particularly legal and financials) is a secular tailwind. That should provide some cushion against an upcoming spending cut regime. Whether Intapp's secular tailwind is deemed mission-critical or not will dictate how it navigates upcoming near term challenges. While 2025 guidance (with just a quarter out ending June 2025) is not reflecting any material change in outlook, 2026 guidance will be critical. If not a pressure downwards, expect some slowdown or stagnation in topline growth as new acquisitions suffer and existing customers resist upselling or even cut down costs.

Buy With Fuel in Tank

While Intapp's long-term growth ceiling is still quite far, as seen in the valuation analysis and strong business potential, the immediate macro environment introduces uncertainty around IT budgets and discretionary spend. This could create more attractive entry points in the near term, though valuations are not clearly stretched or compressed enough to suggest how much future risk or upside is already priced in. Prices today appear fairly valued to Intapp's fundamentals today. New bets will be on growth and profitability from here on.

Decelerating revenue growth is a concern. But high gross margins, operational discipline and AI-enhanced SaaS offerings are structural drivers for future expansion. Investors can consider some exposure to Intapp as timing the perfect entry point is difficult. Expect gains as Intapp enters consistent profitability, and be ready to accumulate on further dips. In terms of risks, keep an eye on consistent EBITDA struggles. The upcoming macro headwind could pressurize topline growth, but accumulate on further dips, unless Intapp faces a secular fall in fundamentals.

data.title: June ECB Meeting: In A Good Place To Assess The Risks
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist; Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist; and Magdalena Ocampo, Market Strategist

As expected, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut its policy rates yesterday for the eighth time in this cycle, marking its seventh straight cut. The interest rates on the main refinancing operations, the marginal lending facility, and the deposit facility were each lowered by 25 basis points to 2.15%, 2.40% and 2.00%, respectively.

With the cumulative easing since June last year now totaling 200bps, and with inflation showing increased signs of stabilizing towards its target, ECB President Christine Lagarde noted that the central bank is in a good position to face uncertainties ahead, notably on evolving U.S. trade policy.

While Lagarde kept the door open for additional rate cuts, the ECB has likely earned some leeway on responding to materializing trade policy risks, especially with increasing fiscal spending providing a significant offset.

Recent developments

While risks to growth remain tilted to the downside amid the ongoing trade war, several factors are keeping Eurozone growth resilient, including a solid labor market, robust balance sheets, and easing financial conditions.

A pull-forward of activity ahead of the U.S. tariffs has also buoyed growth in Q1 and is likely to provide some momentum through the rest of the year. Moreover, there have also been increased signs that inflation is likely to stabilize and move closer towards the ECB’s 2% target as wage pressures continue to ease.

As a result, ECB President Lagarde has characterized that the ECB is well-positioned to navigate the uncertainties ahead. While she left the door open for additional rate cuts, she acknowledged that the ECB is getting close to the end of the monetary policy easing cycle.

Additional rate cuts will likely depend on the evolution of the economy amid trade policy risks materializing in the months ahead, particularly with ongoing trade negotiations between the EU and the U.S. Indeed, trade policy is likely the largest source of uncertainty to policymakers currently, particularly given the erratic nature of U.S. trade policy.

Forecast changes

The updated ECB staff projections showed a notable downgrade to inflation in 2026, driven by a combination of lower energy prices and a stronger euro. Inflation is now projected to grow 1.6% in 2026, down from the previous 1.9% forecast, and 2027 inflation is forecasted to grow 2%. These projections suggest that the ECB has greater visibility and conviction on inflation reaching its 2% target on a sustained basis.

ECB growth projections were modestly downgraded for 2026, with GDP expected to grow 1.1%, slightly down from the previous 1.2%, as trade policy uncertainty is likely to further weigh on economic activity. However, the fiscal spending boost - particularly on defense and infrastructure - should provide significant stimulus to offset the negative impact of tariffs and support growth going forward.

Policy outlook

With economic activity holding up better than expected, and with inflation showing increasing signs of stabilizing closer towards the ECB’s target, the central bank is in a good position to assess incoming risks before embarking on additional rate cuts.

Investors should not get too complacent with encouraging signs from the ECB, however, as trade policy uncertainty remains extremely elevated and is likely to continue to drive the economic outlook and sentiment going forward. Therefore, while the door remains open for additional easing from the ECB this year, they may no longer come at consecutive meetings.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: FTXR: Tesla Now A Fixture For This Factor-Based Transportation ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The First Trust Nasdaq Transportation ETF (NASDAQ:FTXR) has been a poor performer since its launch in September 2016, but an adjustment to its selection and weighting process in March 2023 and the emergence of Tesla (TSLA) as a key player in the automotive space makes FTXR a much different product moving forward. Though a change was desperately needed, this article explains why I'm not confident these were the right changes, and after comparing it with four other close peers, there doesn't appear to be a great opportunity today. Therefore, I've assigned it a "hold" rating, and I look forward to explaining why in further detail below.

FTXR Overview

Strategy Discussion

FTXR tracks the Nasdaq US Smart Transportation Index. First Trust provides a description of the selection process on its website, but unfortunately, the description is not accurate. I've contacted First Trust numerous times about this discrepancy for other ETFs tracking Nasdaq US Smart Indexes but have not heard back. As a workaround, I went straight to the source and provided the following summary based on the Index methodology document.

1. The selection universe is all stocks in the following subsectors, per the Industry Classification Benchmark system:

  • Auto Services
  • Tires
  • Automobiles
  • Auto Parts
  • Airlines
  • Trucking
  • Commercial Vehicles and Parts
  • Railroads
  • Railroad Equipment
  • Delivery Services
  • Commercial Vehicle-Equipment Leasing
  • Transportation Services

The first five subsectors are in the Consumer Discretionary industry, and the last seven are in the Industrials industry.

2. The Index universe is narrowed to those with at least $2.5 billion in total market capitalization and $5 million three-month average daily traded value. At least 30 securities must pass this stage before the screen is relaxed, first by market cap and then by liquidity.

3. The Index ranks remaining securities based on an aggregate composite of four statistics: gross income, return on assets, a price momentum composite (3, 6, 9, and 12 months), and cash flow less stock option expenses.

4. The Index selects securities ranking within the top 75% of the Index universe, subject to a 30-stock minimum and 50-stock maximum.

5. The Index weights based on a security's cash flow less stock option expense, subject to conditions that generally limit allocations to between 0.5% and 8.0% at each reconstitution date in March and September.

Prior to March 20, 2023, the Index selected securities based on volatility, value, and growth, based on this archived version of the same document. In addition, weights were based on a composite of these three factors instead of the current cash flow-driven approach. It's crucial that readers realize the enormous implications of these changes, especially now that Tesla (TSLA) has generated nearly $17 billion in operating cash flow over the last twelve months. That figure was -$124 million when FTXR launched, and since that's the key determinant of a security's weight, today's components look nothing like they did in 2016.

Performance Summary

Change is needed, too, because FTXR has been a poor performer since its launch, delivering only a 77.83% total return compared to 106.58% and 82.09% for the iShares Transportation Average ETF (IYT) and the SPDR S&P Transportation ETF (XTN), respectively.

Transportation stocks have performed badly against the broader Industrials sector, evidenced by the substantially higher returns for the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight Industrials ETF (RSPN) and the Industrial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLI). However, in a piece of good news for FTXR, it has outperformed its most direct peers (IYT and XTN) since its Index updated its rules. A lot has to do with Tesla (TSLA), which gained a remarkable 62.52% in 2024.

FTXR Analysis

Composition Analysis

As I mentioned earlier, FTXR's auto stocks are classified in the Consumer Discretionary sector, which is not permitted in IYT, XTN, RSPN, and XLI. The breakdown between Consumer Discretionary and Industrials is 33/67%, and it just so happens that FTXR's top three stocks are in the automobile industry, which comprises 26.81% of the portfolio.

Fundamentals By Top 25 Holdings

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for FTXR's top 25 holdings, which total 91.74%. Have a quick scan of these statistics, and I'll offer my takeaways afterward.

1. FTXG's weighted average operating cash flow over the last twelve months is $8.6 billion, the highest of the five ETFs. The table nicely illustrates that this figure determines each stock's weighting in the Index, but more importantly, notice how Tesla, Ford Motor (F), and General Motors (GM) generate the most cash flow by far. To be specific, they combine for 39% of the total operating cash flow of all members, so all they have to do is merely qualify for the Index, and they'll receive the maximum 8% weighting at each reconstitution. They'll easily do that, too, given how cash flow is also one of the four factors used to determine which stocks qualify.

2. FTXG's momentum screens are at least partially on display. For example, its constituents are up 12.24% over the last year on a weighted average basis, and numerous stocks, including Tesla (TSLA), United Airlines (UAL), and Ryder System (R), contribute to this strong statistic. IYT's and XTN's one-year price returns are only 6.46% and 1.89%, respectively, but these pale in comparison to RSPN and XLI's figures. Furthermore, I'm not sold on the effectiveness of the factor as applied to such a small group of stocks, and I think it would be more useful to assess momentum compared to a broad-based sector or market benchmark.

3. FTXG trades at 12.30x forward earnings and 4.85x trailing cash flow, indicating it's the best value-oriented fund of the five. However, its one-year estimated sales and growth rates are only about 3%, so it's also the worst from a growth perspective. I also confirmed this by calculating sector-adjusted value and growth scores for each ETF, which I derived from their components' Seeking Alpha Factor Grades, as follows (value, growth score):

  • FTXR: 5.71/10, 3.50/10
  • IYT: 5.18/10, 5.05/10
  • XTN: 6.37/10, 5.33/10
  • RSPN: 2.72/10, 4.53/10
  • XLI: 2.50/10, 4.82/10

When combining the two scores, IYT and XTN look superior, but I caution that the entire transportation sector is priced low for a good reason. For example, sales surprises were virtually flat for the components in FTXR, IYT, and XTN last quarter (0.15% / 0.06% / -0.74%), and I suggest that as the source of their poor momentum compared to the broader Industrials sector, where sales surprises averaged 0.66%. Weighing on FTXR's poor result is Tesla, whose 9.34% miss was the biggest in the last two years.

Since Tesla is virtually guaranteed to earn the maximum 8% weight in FTXR at each semi-annual reconstitution, that makes the fund riskier than the other two transportation ETFs, which, by design, aren't permitted to hold the stock.

Investment Recommendation

FTXR tracks a specialty Index designed to select 30-50 transportation stocks based on gross income, return on assets, price momentum, and cash flow. Unique to FTXR is its inclusion of auto stocks Tesla, Ford, and General Motors, and since its components are weighted based on cash flow, their allocations will max out at 8% at each semi-annual reconstitution. Especially in the case of Tesla, this suggests FTXR will be an above-average risky product for the foreseeable future.

Unfortunately, momentum is not with transportation stocks at the moment, and peers like IYT and XTN have also lagged the broader Industrials sector for the better part of the last nine years. FTXR's growth rates are also quite low, so despite its attractive 12.30x forward P/E, I don't see a great investment opportunity today. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Amgen's Strategic Path - Navigating Patent Expiries With Promising New Therapies
data.description:
Introduction

My last look at Amgen (NASDAQ:AMGN) was in March. My focus was on its Phase 3 obesity treatment, MariTide, which cleverly combines GLP-1 agonism with GIP antagonism in a once-monthly injection. MariTide is critical to Amgen's future, as nearly 30% of its revenue is "at risk" within the next five years.

The following article reviews Amgen's most recent financial results, its "revenue at risk," and investigational drugs. I also conducted a discounted cash flow analysis that reveals Amgen is priced for no growth, implying substantial upside should the company outperform low expectations.

Recent Developments

Amgen's portfolio is diversified, with multiple blockbuster drugs.

Here are its top performers from 2024:

Drug FY2024 Sales (in $ millions) YOY Growth (%) Primary Patent Expiry
Prolia 4,374 8 US patent expired Feb. 2025; biosimilar launches expected H2 2025
Enbrel 3,316 (10) US patents extend to 2028/2029; ongoing antitrust litigation; existing price erosion
Xgeva 2,225 5 US patent expired Feb. 2025; biosimilar launches expected H2 2025
Repatha 2,222 36 No immediate patent threat
Otezla 2,126 (3) US patents extend to 2028; impairment charge taken in Q1 2025

Notably, the top three drugs, which account for nearly $10 billion of Amgen's $33.4 billion total revenue in 2024, are vulnerable to patent cliffs.

First Quarter 2025 Performance

In its first quarter 2025 earnings report, Amgen saw its revenues grow 9% year-over-year to $8.1 billion. Beyond looming patent cliffs, Amgen is also dealing with a reduction in net selling prices. This has been an ongoing issue (see trend in "Gross Margin" card in the first image above). So, 14% volume growth was partially offset by a 6% lower net selling price. Still, Amgen's GAAP operating income improved by 1.1 percentage points to 15%. Enbrel, once a top product of Amgen, is feeling the pain. Its sales decreased 10% year-over-year in the first quarter due to a 47% drop in net selling price.

Looking ahead, Amgen projects 2025 revenues to range between $34.3 billion and $35.7 billion, with an acceleration in operating margin in subsequent quarters.

Strategic Acquisitions

Much of Amgen's growth can be attributed to its $27.8 billion acquisition of Horizon in 2023. Tepezza, for Thyroid Eye Disease, amassed $1.9 billion in 2024 sales. Its first quarter performance was more muted ($381 million, down 10% year-over-year) due to inventory adjustments. While Tepezza is trending well, relying on acquisitions to drive growth has its setbacks (see "Financial Flexibility" card).

Pipeline Progress

Thanks to solid "innovation investments," Amgen does have a few tricks up its sleeve. MariTide remains in Phase 3 for obesity without diabetes and Phase 2 for obesity with diabetes. Amgen anticipates data from Part 2 of the Phase 2 chronic weight management study later this year. Some believe MariTide could generate as much as $5 billion in peak annual revenue, which would go a long way in offsetting some revenue at risk.

Another interesting developmental drug is olpasiran, a "small interfering RNA that reduces lipoprotein(a) synthesis in the liver." Phase 2 data, published in NEJM, demonstrated the drug's ability to significantly reduce lipoprotein(a) in patients with established heart disease with subcutaneous administration every 12 weeks. Lipoprotein(a) is believed to be a "causal risk factor for atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease," and there is no currently available drug that addresses this. There are multiple drugs targeting lipoprotein(a) in development. Ionis' (IONS) once-monthly pelacarsen appears furthest in development, with Phase 3 trial data expected in the first half of 2026. It is estimated that around one in five people have elevated lipoprotein(a). This could be a multi-billion-dollar market for drugmakers, much like the $9.3 billion PCSK9 inhibitor market, which also addresses lipid and cardiovascular disease. Amgen anticipates initiating a Phase 3 trial for olpasiran within a year.

Biosimilar Initiatives

Amgen is also making investments in its biosimilar business. This is a lower-margin business compared to brand drugs, but it helps diversify revenue streams. Notably, Amgen recently launched the first FDA-approved biosimilar of Stelara for the treatment of multiple inflammatory conditions, Wezlana. It generated $150 million in first-quarter sales. Amgen is also developing biosimilars for Opdivo, Keytruda, and Ocrevus (multiple sclerosis). These are all blockbuster opportunities, but, like I said, lower margins, and there will likely be several biosimilars vying for market share. So, it's not like Wezlana is bound for glory.

Valuation Analysis and Sensitivity

Given these dynamics, it's no surprise that AMGN is conservatively valued on a cash flow basis. My discounted cash flow (DCF) model suggests that AMGN is priced for no growth.

I used revenue growth and FCF margin assumptions of 2.5% and 30%, respectively. In the trailing twelve months, Amgen's FCF margin was 33.3% (CFO less CapEx). Here are analyst revenue estimates on AMGN:

So, despite billions of dollars at risk, analysts anticipate that Amgen will have stable growth. Bear in mind that my assumptions represent averages over the nine-year projection period. Amgen's beta is lower per source, but I went with a blended one to be conservative. Everything else was gathered from SEC filings/standard DCF methodology.

Even with conservative assumptions (I'm essentially assuming that margins will continue to narrow and that Amgen has reached terminal growth), Amgen is undervalued by about 27%. This is not uncommon for large pharmaceutical companies facing large patent cliffs. Amgen's future growth relies heavily on investigational treatments like MariTide, and the outcomes of such drugs are inherently uncertain.

My sensitivity analysis also acts like a "reverse DCF model," in that it reveals the implied market assumptions of growth (assuming all other inputs remain the same).

Amgen appears priced for -0.4% revenue growth, with significant upside should it achieve mid-single-digit growth.

If you are convinced that Amgen can sustain low-single-digit growth in the next decade, the risk/reward profile is highly favorable.

Conclusion

To wrap up, Amgen faces the same problems many large pharma companies do, including inflation, drug price pressures, tariffs, and patent cliffs. The headwinds are significant, and it's no wonder that AMGN and its peers are underperforming the market. However, investigational drugs like MariTide and olpasiran offer compelling potential, and Amgen is priced for no growth. Should it exceed low expectations or have breakthroughs in the clinic, I can envision upside potential, meriting continued speculation (maintain Buy).

data.title: THOR Industries: Accelerating With Caution After The Potholes
data.description:
THOR Industries, Inc. (NYSE:THO) has experienced softer market conditions in the past two years. In my previous coverage, I already discussed how it had to endure a tough FY23 and FY24. On a lighter note, it exuded resilience through cost and expense management to stay profitable and sustainable.

At this point, we are slowly seeing the fruit of its labor with its notable improvement in the most recent quarter. The RV market remains quite weaker than its 2021-2022 high, but its trajectory is a positive one. By the looks of it, RV companies like THO may be on their way to recovery. However, they may still watch out for macroeconomic risks today.

With respect to the price, the stock appears more fairly valued now than in my previous coverage. Technicals are weak, but buying opportunities are easier to find.

THO Q3 2025: A Gradual Recovery

After quarters of weak performances, THOR Industries, Inc. has finally regained its strength to prove its durability, Its Q3 2025 operating revenue amounted to $2.9B, up by 3.3% YoY from $2.8B. It was also 43% higher than in Q2 2025. While we can attribute this large increase to seasonality, its improvement was visible. From Q1 2024 to Q2 2025, THO experienced a YoY revenue decrease. After six quarters, THO was able to enjoy revenue growth once again.

North American Towable RVs remained its primary growth driver, with a 9.1% YoY increase. Its shipments also increased by 5.5% or 1,883 units, showing increased demand. This was also the segment where THO really had true recovery. Aside from a higher sales volume, THO enjoyed an increase in the average price from $31,334 to $32,400. In addition, its gross profit margin increased by 2.0 bps. For its North American Motorized RVs, THO drove demand using its pricing strategy. Shipments rose by 10.9% or 543 units, while the average price dropped from $130,327 to $121,062. Nonetheless, it still worked, as sales rose by 3.1% YoY.

Overall, I think what really drove THO was prudent product diversification and inventory management. Its increased focus on Towable RVs allows it to capitalize on what it does best. It is in line with the market demand, so focusing on it also helps it improve its sales and margins. I will discuss this more later. The revenue increase in this segment completely offsets the weakness in its European RV segment.

This move also helped it improve its cost and expense management. As a result, its operating leverage slightly weakened from 90.2% to 90.0%, showing its well-managed cost sensitivity. Its gross profit margin improved from 15.1% to 15.3%. Lastly, its net income margin rose from 4.1% to 4.6%. If we make the income tax expenses the same as the previous year, it will decrease to 4.4%. This will still be higher, showing improved profitability.

New Tariffs Versus Lower Oil Prices

These are external aspects that may influence macroeconomic conditions and THO's performance.

Higher tariffs can raise THO's cost pressures and affect margins in the long run. Why? Increasing tariffs also increase the cost of importing auto parts and components. Retaliation may also take place. So, without proper implementation and plans, THO may have a lower pricing flexibility to cover extra expenses.

On a lighter note, I think lower oil prices can partially offset tariffs. For instance, WTI and Brent move at the 52-week low. This downtrend may be sustained by the OPEC supply hike this July. Considering this, oil prices may stay below $70 per barrel. As a result, shipping and transportation may benefit from these positive spillovers. In addition, RV travel may have renewed demand as lower oil prices may also lower fuel expenses. This can help American travelers save more money by travelling domestically instead of by plane. In a recent survey, 44M of Americans will do RV travel this summer.

RV Recovery

The RV industry is recovering from its weak condition in 2023-2024. Pricing remains a key driver, especially for motorhomes, since these are more expensive. We saw this in my computation of the average price per unit of THO's motorhome RVs. That is why we saw a slight improvement in motorhome shipments last March. It was the first time that motorhome shipments had a positive YoY change. In April, it weakened again by -3.4%. Yet, it was better than in previous months, mostly a double-digit YoY decrease. Meanwhile, Towable RVs are the crown jewel of THO and the whole industry. Total shipments rose by 4.2% YoY to 31,982 units. Hence, total shipments still increased by 3.4% YoY from 34,197 to 35,375 RVs.

This positive may be sustained in the second half as summer trips highlight the popularity of RVing. This summer, 42% of all planned trips will be road trips. What's even more interesting is that RV's popularity has already been passed down from generation to generation. Millennials are taking the lead, with a 40% probability of purchasing RVs in 2026. Meanwhile, Gen Zers topped the list of online searches for RVs with 45% of the total number.

Another potential catalyst is the increased preference for remote work and learning. So, parents and their children can travel together while working and studying. In addition, travelling by RV can be a cheaper option than international trips or beaches. A survey shows that over 60% of Americans won't travel this summer because they can't afford it. Among those who will travel, the majority choose domestic trips.

Liquidity Remains Excellent

Aside from these market trends, THO capitalizes on its strong liquidity to ensure sustainability. As we can see, its cash has increased considerably in the past 11 quarters to $508M. One factor to consider is its increased profitability, driven by its strategic cost and expense management. If we look at its cash flow statement, THO has maintained a positive FCF in the past five quarters. In Q3 2025, it generated an FCF of $224M, which means that it converted 8% of its sales into free cash. Another crucial aspect is its effective inventory management. THO carefully produced RVs corresponding to the market demand. The decreasing trend of inventory shows three things. First, it produced less to balance its demand and supply. Second, it produced less to manage costs and expenses better. Third, demand is starting to recover.

More interestingly, its borrowings have considerably decreased. This shows that RVs has been generating enough cash inflows and/or earnings to repay its borrowings. In fact, its cash can cover half of its borrowings in a single payment. With its Net Debt/EBITDA of 0.84x, THO only needs about 10 months to cover all borrowings at once. This means that it has enough time and resources to prepare for its repayments.

Valuation

THO has already dropped by -10.4% since my previous coverage, in line with my hold rating. At its current price, THO appears more reasonable and fairly valued. We can assess its valuation relative to key metrics, such as BVPS and Sales Per Share or SPS. If we use the P/B, the price is now trading at 1.37x its BVPS, which is cheaper than the five-year average of 1.54x. This suggests a TP of $96.93, a 13% upside potential. A similar trend can be seen in its P/S Ratio, although it leads to a lower TP of $88.50, a 3% upside potential.

Moreover, the price of THO is already more realistic if we check the DCF Model I used in my previous coverage. With my TP of $90.43, the model gives a 5% upside. Hence, buying at its current price is already reasonable.

Technicals

THO has shown improvements in its momentum since its correction. While its bullish signals remain weak, we can see its continued recovery. The price keeps increasing, while the 50-day SMA has already flattened, showing a potential shift in market sentiments. Yet, we may need more confirmation to confirm bullish signals. One indication is when the price crosses above the 200-day SMA. Another is when the 50-day SMA moves up again.

For MACD and RSI, we cannot detect any bullish or bearish divergence yet. THO has been consolidating in the past three weeks. The sudden price jump yesterday was due to the Q3 2025 results, but the trading day ended with sellers still pulling the price down from $89-91 to $84-86.

It is also worth noting that before the Q3 release, the consolidation happens slightly below the 0.382 Fibonacci Retracement, which suggests testing of the first resistance at $82, which has already been broken a few times. So, the price jump could be a normal reaction as buyers tried to push the price up to the 0.50 retracement point before decreasing again. Considering this trend, the price may retrace to $83-84 again as momentum improves. It is also interesting to note that the 0.50 retracement point is also very close to my DCF TP. Hence, the current price is already a good entry point.

Conclusion

THOR Industries, Inc. remains a solid company amid the gradual market recovery. Its prudent product mix and inventory management allow it to stay profitable and enjoy strong cash flows. It is also fairly valued today, with a decent upside potential. In addition, technicals show some buying opportunities, making the price an ideal entry point. I am upgrading my rating on this stock from hold to buy.

data.title: Broadcom's Post-Q2 Earnings Pullback Is A Classic Overreaction
data.description:
Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) reported 2QFY25; sales grew 20% Y/Y to $15B, more or less in line with estimates of $14.97B, and guidance for 5% sequential growth next quarter to $15.8B in sales was once again in line with estimates. In line is basically a miss in terms of market reaction; so, AVGO stock is down almost 5% in extended trading on guidance not surpassing estimates materially. Heading into print, the stock was on a run; in fact, it was the best-performing semi name in our coverage since 2QCY25 began, up 55% since the end of March and almost 30% on the 1M chart. So, while AVGO continues to dominate the AI custom ASIC business and outperforms expectations with its networking ICs pushing up AI revenues, the stock still dropped. This is what we like to call an illogical sell-off, or a gift. We're upgrading Broadcom to a strong buy on what is shaping up to be a post-earnings sell off and reset expectations.

The above showcases AVGO stock movement post-earnings in extended trading and the stock run-up since we last said buy in early April on the Investing Experts Podcast with Rena, since which the stock is up 66% versus the S&P 500 up 19%. We believe the post-earnings sell off Friday will be a rare window to add this stock.

Pay Attention to What Matters

For the quarter, AVGO reported semi sales up 2.4% Q/Q and 17% Y/Y to $8.41B supported by their AI-related demand and saw AI revenues specifically grow 7% Q/Q to $4.4B. Non-AI semi sales were weaker sequentially, down 2% to $4B. This isn't a big scare, however, because it's just the result of seasonal weakness from Apple (AAPL), which, let's not forget, makes up roughly 20% of AVGO's total sales. On the other end, infrastructure software sales were down 1.6% Q/Q to $6.6B. That's the quick overview.

What investors should pay attention to here, in our opinion, is the fact that AI revenues surged 46% Y/Y and that wasn't on ASIC alone, it was on AI networking demand, which, we believe, is AVGO's weapon along with its software side of the business. Why are these Broadcom's weapons? Because they'll allow the company to scale its ASIC business without squeezing margins. AI custom ASIC is a low-margin business; just last month, AVGO's close competitor in the ASIC business, Marvell (MRVL), said on their earnings call that

...within custom, there is a range, and it's like the old adage in semiconductors. The higher the volume, you're going to see lower gross margins.

And while MRVL's gross margins have been on decline for the past five consecutive quarters, AVGO's margins actually expanded. For the quarter, AVGO's non-GAAP gross margins expanded 30 basis points to 79.4%. AVGO's infrastructure software business and its AI networking offset the lower margin ASIC business. And, we expect margins to continue to expand with management guiding for infrastructure software sales to grow to $6.7B next quarter from $6.6B this quarter and for AI revenue to grow 16% Q/Q to $5.1B due to continued networking demand filling through.

What about ASIC? In our opinion, AVGO will easily be the industry's go-to for tier 1 hyperscalers; we're talking Google (GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT). While MRVL also has some of these on its customer list, AVGO is the more reliable pick between the two because it has economies of scale and resources. Either way, we think tier 1 players won't stick to being single source, but we see AVGO benefiting most from the ASIC peers. The company now has three main customers: Google, Meta and, supposedly, ByteDance, TikTok's parent company, while overall, it has announced five customers and hinted at two new prospects to come. Even just looking at the top three, AVGO appears to be in good hands, considering these are big players. Google is doubling down on spending $75B in CapEx for the year, and Meta just raised CapEx this quarter from ~$60-65B to $64B to $72B. AVGO designed next-gen TPUv7 3nm for Google and is working on TPUv8 2nm and co-designed MTIA AI chips for Meta and is sampling next-gen 3nm MTIA chip. The chart below outlines a bit of Broadcom's competitive pipeline versus MRVL, and it also provides ramp timing, which shows AVGO has a longer roadmap secured than MRVL.

Management isn't hiding its cards, which is why the stock has run up so much and why AVGO saw 34 upward revenue revisions over the past three months and only three downward ones. All these factors made the risk-reward less attractive heading into print but all the more attractive now. We're expecting demand for ASIC to expand further into next year because mid and low complexity AI workloads will begin to be offloaded from AI GPUs onto ASIC. The reason is that tier 1 guys need to reduce their reliance on Nvidia (NVDA) and lower costs by making a longer-term in-house solution for inference workloads. This is done through ASIC. Although we probably won't see ASIC make a dent in demand for NVDA AI GPUs in the near to mid-term, it'll be the future for inference and one in which AVGO is well positioned. We think the stock falling on this quarter's report makes for a good reset of expectations post-rally and another window to add.

Valuation

AVGO is pricey, and after this quarter's sales and guidance for Q3 are coming in line, investors are obviously worried about whether it's worth the price. We strongly think it is. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 58.35x compared to a group average of 28.38x, and on an EV/Sales basis, the stock trades at 20.25x versus an average of 2.82x. The chart below shows the full scoop of the financials.

Bottom Line: Limited Opportunity to Add Post-reset

While the stock is expensive, we think the premium multiples are there for a reason: this is a growth stock. AVGO's revenue growth is 40% compared to a sector average of 6%, with a forward EPS growth projection of 23% compared to a sector median of 10%. Profitability is also well secured, with an EBITDA margin of 77% versus the sector's average of -3%. AVGO is an AI name and one of the few ones that is getting an expectation reset at the moment, and its AI revenue expectations are manageable; we see a longer-term opportunity for investors to buy the panic. We know we've been waiting for an opportunity like this for AVGO because the stock was on an upward trajectory and within overbought territory since the 9th of May.

data.title: Is It Ethereum's Time Again? ETH And ETH/BTC Technical Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Ethereum (ETH-USD) had a rough beginning of 2025 - failing to reach new all-time highs, while Bitcoin (BTC-USD) smashed through its own ATH multiple times. Solana (SOL-USD), providing a cheaper alternative to ETH services, also held stronger than the Ether throughout the latter part of 2024 and beginning 2025.

ETH performance is key to the overall crypto market performance; the past Altcoin cycles have always been led by ETH performance over BTC.

ETH/BTC is a great cryptocurrency spread for a crypto trader to spot relative performance, provide a direction for which crypto to choose, and track the appetite for altcoins.

Through this crypto market update, you will see how ETH/BTC rising helped altcoin bull runs in the past cycle, something that many crypto traders have been awaiting and is yet to materialize again.

ETH/BTC and its Correlation to total Market Performance

This 2017-2021 chart of ETH/BTC contains essential information about understanding the crypto market.

Through 1 and 2, we observe how the first bull run in ETH and ETH/BTC led to the 2017 total crypto market bull run - taking the market cap from $20-30B average to highs of $620B in November 2017.

Number 3 on the chart shows how a drop in ETH/BTC leads to a significant cooldown of the crypto market, going back towards a total market cap between $100 and $250B from 2018 to the end of 2020.

From end-2020 to the end of 2021, number 4 shows the same correlation of ETH/BTC and crypto markets rallying.

This Covid-era bull run introduced crypto for an ever-bigger number of investors, and led to the flurry of altcoins and cryptocurrency projects such as Doge, Avax, Solana. The crypto market cap went from $300B to $2.86T, and this level only got reached again in November 2024.

Finally, number 5 shows similarly to number 3 how the drop in market cap correlates with a drop in the ETH/BTC spread.

ETH/BTC from 2020 to Today

We take a closer look at the 2021 Bull Run and how the December 2021 top in ETH/BTC led again a significant correction in the crypto market.

2023 was all about Bitcoin Dominance as its rally from $14,475 to its record highs of $112,030 left Ethereum and all other altcoins lagging.

We are seeing a breakout from the descent that started at the same level as the 2020 ETH/BTC bullish breakout - we will see if the spread continues upward and if it generates another bull run for altcoins.

ETH Daily Chart

ETH had a consequent rally after a significant drop between $4,000 highs in December 2024 to $1,363 lows in April 2025.

Prices are consolidating between $2,300 and $2,600. The key is to see if Bitcoin prices that are also stagnating above the $100,000 Mark leads to rallies in other cryptos as the same phenomenon happened in past bull cycles.

Levels to watch:

Support Zones:

  • S1: 2,385 to 2,525
  • S2: 2,035 to 2,167
  • S3: 1,700 to 1,825

Resistance Zones:

  • R1: 2,600 to 2,750
  • R2: 3,225 to 3,363
  • R3: 3,660 to 3,800

ETH 4H Chart

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
